article_meta.title: Sign up for Executive Dysfunction, a weekly newsletter that surfaces under-the-radar stories about what Trump is doing t

reasoning: The model does a reasonable job identifying genuine bias in the author's prose and mostly avoids flagging quoted text. However, there are some issues: one item flags language that is arguably part of the interview transcript (Fidell's quoted speech about Hegseth), and the model misses several additional clear bias instances in the author's own prose. The bias type labels are mostly defensible.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: The fourth flagged item ('a fact that he has demonstrated time and time again in office') is from Eugene Fidell's speech in the interview transcript, not the author's own prose. The article is structured as an interview, and this line appears within Fidell's response. This is an attribution rule violation, though not egregious since the interview format makes the boundary between author and source somewhat blurry. The other three items are genuinely from the author's introductory prose.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: The first three items are defensible genuine bias patterns in the author's own introductory prose. The fourth item is an attribution violation (Fidell's words). So 1 out of 4 items is a false positive due to attribution error. The remaining three are reasonable flags.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model missed several clear bias instances in the author's own prose: 'freewheeling authority to commence hostilities' (Word Choice), 'This theory of executive supremacy leaves American armed forces at the whim of one man—a total inversion of our constitutional design' (Opinion Presented as Fact/Sensationalism), 'a flagrant abuse of office' used in the author's framing (though this is describing Fidell's argument, so borderline). The author's introduction is heavily loaded with opinion language and the model caught some but not all significant instances.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: The labels are mostly defensible. 'Opinion Statements Presented as Fact' works for the first two items. 'Subjective Qualifying Adjectives' is correct for 'startling.' The fourth item's label of 'Negativity Bias' is debatable but not clearly wrong (though it's moot since it's an attribution violation). Word Choice could also work for the first item. Overall the type labels are reasonable.
